era_context: The late 16th century was a period of religious conflict in Europe, state consolidation in Asia, and accelerating European colonial expansion. The Ottoman Empire remained a major Mediterranean and Eastern European power. The Ming Dynasty ruled China under the Wanli Emperor. Japan was in the final years of Toyotomi Hideyoshi's regency. The Americas were experiencing rapid Spanish colonisation with devastating demographic consequences for indigenous populations.

events.title: French Wars of Religion: Battle of Ivry
events.description: The Battle of Ivry, fought on 14 March 1590 (old style) or 1591 (new style), was a major engagement during the French Wars of Religion. Henry IV's forces defeated the Catholic League forces commanded by the Duke of Mayenne. The battle was a significant victory for Henry IV and demonstrated his growing military strength. The victory at Ivry was followed by the Siege of Paris (1590–1591), in which Henry IV attempted to capture the capital from Catholic League forces. Though Henry IV did not capture Paris in 1591, the battle and siege demonstrated his ability to challenge Catholic dominance in France.

sources.name: Contemporary military chronicles and court records

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ivry is well-documented in contemporary sources. The dating varies slightly depending on whether the old or new calendar is used (March 1590 old style = March 1591 new style).

events.title: Siege of Paris during the French Wars of Religion
events.description: Henry IV's forces besieged Paris from 1590 to 1591, attempting to capture the capital from Catholic League forces. The siege lasted approximately one year and resulted in significant hardship for the city's population, including famine and disease. However, the arrival of Spanish forces under the Duke of Parma relieved the siege, and Henry IV was forced to withdraw. The failure to capture Paris in 1591 demonstrated the continued strength of Catholic resistance and the complexity of Henry IV's path to undisputed kingship. The siege would eventually be resolved through Henry IV's conversion to Catholicism in 1593 and the subsequent Edict of Nantes in 1598.

sources.name: Contemporary military chronicles and eyewitness accounts

events.certainty_note: The Siege of Paris is well-documented in contemporary sources from multiple perspectives.

events.title: Toyotomi Hideyoshi's consolidation of power in Japan
events.description: Toyotomi Hideyoshi, the regent of Japan, continued his consolidation of power and preparation for the invasion of Korea (which would begin in 1592). In 1591, Hideyoshi was in his mid-50s and at the height of his power. He had unified Japan through military conquest and was now focused on maintaining control and preparing for his ambitious foreign campaigns. Hideyoshi's administrative reforms and military organisation had transformed Japan from a fragmented feudal state into a more centralised polity. The year 1591 marked the final preparations for the Korean invasion.
events.certainty_note: The broad context of Hideyoshi's consolidation is well-established, though specific events in 1591 are less precisely dated in contemporary sources.

events.title: Portuguese colonial activities in India and Southeast Asia
events.description: Portuguese colonial forces in India and Southeast Asia continued their military campaigns and commercial activities. The Portuguese had established a significant presence in the Indian Ocean following Vasco da Gama's voyage (1498) and had developed a network of trading posts and fortified settlements. In the 1590s, the Portuguese were engaged in military campaigns against local rulers and competing European powers. The Portuguese maintained control of key ports like Goa and Cochin and were expanding their influence in Southeast Asia, particularly in the Spice Islands.
key_figures: Portuguese colonial governors and military commanders

sources.name: Portuguese colonial records and military reports

events.certainty_note: Portuguese colonial activities in the 1590s are documented in Portuguese records, though specific events in 1591 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty military operations and administrative reforms
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Wanli Emperor continued its military operations against Jurchen tribes and Mongol incursions along the northern frontier. The Ming maintained a large standing army and navy, though resources were increasingly strained by military expenditures. The 1590s saw ongoing administrative reforms and efforts to strengthen the Ming military and bureaucracy. The Wanli Emperor, though increasingly withdrawn from direct governance, maintained nominal authority over the empire.

sources.name: Ming court records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: The broad context of Ming military and administrative activities is well-documented, though specific events in 1591 are less precisely dated.

events.title: English privateering and colonial ventures
events.description: English privateers and explorers, operating under the patronage of Queen Elizabeth I, continued raids on Spanish shipping and colonial possessions. The 1590s saw English privateers conducting successful raids on Spanish treasure fleets and colonial settlements in the Caribbean and along the coasts of the Americas. These privateering ventures were sanctioned by the English crown and served both economic and strategic purposes. The period also saw continued English interest in North American colonisation, though earlier ventures like the Roanoke Colony had been abandoned.

sources.name: English court records and naval chronicles
Spanish colonial records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: English privateering activities in the 1590s are well-documented in contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Henry IV's military dominance in the 1590s can overstate his actual power—the failure to capture Paris in 1591 and the need for his conversion to Catholicism in 1593 demonstrate the continued strength of Catholic resistance. The Portuguese colonial presence in India and Southeast Asia is sometimes portrayed as unchallenged, but it faced significant resistance from local rulers and was eventually challenged by other European powers (Dutch, English). The English privateering narrative can overstate England's naval power relative to Spain, which remained the dominant naval force.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1591 is rich, particularly for European events. French, Spanish, English, and Portuguese sources are abundant and contemporary. Asian sources are well-preserved in Chinese and Japanese records but are less frequently consulted in Western historiography. The historical record is shaped by the dominance of court-sponsored chronicles and the perspective of literate elites. Indigenous perspectives from the Americas and Asia are largely absent from contemporary written sources. The period reflects the early modern transition from medieval to modern state structures, with significant bias toward political and military events over social and economic history.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Ivry and the Siege of Paris were part of the same military campaign
The failure to capture Paris led to Henry IV's conversion to Catholicism
Hideyoshi's consolidation of power led to the invasion of Korea in 1592
